title: A rollout note on feature flags and schema drift
content: The fastest way we found to make feature flags boring again: generate typed contracts from the same schema the backend uses, then fail CI if the fallback path drifts. Half the bugs disappeared before QA ever saw them.
contentHtml:
The fastest way we found to make feature flags boring again: generate typed contracts from the same schema the backend uses, then fail CI if the fallback path drifts.

Half the bugs disappeared before QA ever saw them.
summary: A synthetic social post that reads like a practical engineering thread opener.

title: Why our dashboard redesign stopped fighting the viewport
content: We thought the fix was more breakpoints. It turned out our cards only needed to understand the space inside the dashboard shell. Container queries plus saner defaults cleaned up both the CSS and the component props.
contentHtml:
We thought the fix was more breakpoints.

It turned out our cards only needed to understand the space inside the dashboard shell. Container queries plus saner defaults cleaned up both the CSS and the component props.
summary: A frontend social post that complements the freeform and article seeds with a shorter format.

title: The replica lag metric that earned a permanent spot on our alerts
content: One tiny change paid for itself fast: every read path now records whether it hit primary because replica lag crossed our threshold. It made stale-read incidents explain themselves instead of hiding inside guesswork.
contentHtml:
One tiny change paid for itself fast: every read path now records whether it hit primary because replica lag crossed our threshold.

It made stale-read incidents explain themselves instead of hiding inside guesswork.
summary: A backend-flavored social seed to round out the post mix with short-form content.

title:
I noticed something interesting:

Claude Code auto-adds itself as a co-author on every git commit. Codex doesn't.

That's why you see Claude everywhere on GitHub, but not Codex.

I wonder why OpenAI is not doing that. Feels like an obvious branding strategy OpenAI is skipping.
content:
I noticed something interesting:

Claude Code auto-adds itself as a co-author on every git commit. Codex doesn't.

That's why you see Claude everywhere on GitHub, but not Codex.

I wonder why OpenAI is not doing that. Feels like an obvious branding strategy OpenAI is skipping.
contentHtml: I noticed something interesting: Claude Code auto-adds itself as a co-author on every git commit. Codex doesn't. That's why you see Claude everywhere on GitHub, but not Codex. I wonder why OpenAI is not doing that. Feels like an obvious branding strategy OpenAI is skipping.

title: Do people like this? We don't do this for codex because it exists to help you and it's important that you remain the owner and accountable for your work without AI taking credit. At the same time it does mean that you can't trace how popular codex is among repos.
content: Do people like this? We don't do this for codex because it exists to help you and it's important that you remain the owner and accountable for your work without AI taking credit. At the same time it does mean that you can't trace how popular codex is among repos.
contentHtml: Do people like this? We don't do this for codex because it exists to help you and it's important that you remain the owner and accountable for your work without AI taking credit. At the same time it does mean that you can't trace how popular codex is among repos.
summary: A brief reflection on why Codex (OpenAI's AI coding tool) doesn't add attribution markers to repositories, emphasizing that the human developer should remain the owner and accountable for their work. The trade-off is that Codex usage can't be tracked across repos, unlike some competing AI coding tools that add visible attribution.
